facts_disclosed: Former Marine — 4 years after high school

turns.content: alright so I need to get my mornings under control. Im waking up at 530 but by the time I actually get out the door its like 715 and I have no idea where the time goes

That's almost two hours of drift, which means there's probably 3-4 time sinks hiding in your morning. The usual culprits:

1. **Phone scrolling** — the #1 killer. Even 10 minutes feels like 2.
2. **Decision fatigue** — figuring out what to wear, what to eat, etc.
3. **No set sequence** — doing things in a different order every day means nothing is automatic.

Here's what I'd suggest: for the next 3 mornings, just write down what you actually do and when. Set a timer on your phone every 15 minutes if you have to. Once you see where the time bleeds, you can fix it.

What does your morning look like right now? Walk me through it rough.

alarm goes off at 530. I lay there for a bit. get up, make coffee, check my phone for any messages from customers or my guys. eat something. shower. get dressed. head out. sounds simple but it just stretches

I can already see a couple spots where time is leaking:

- **"Lay there for a bit"** — this is probably 15-20 minutes, not the 5 it feels like.
- **"Check my phone for messages"** — you open it for texts and end up on news, social media, whatever. Another 15-20 easy.
- **"Eat something"** — if you're deciding what to eat and then making it, that's variable.

Here's a tightened version of the same morning:

- 5:30 — Alarm. Feet on floor. No phone.
- 5:35 — Shower first (wakes you up, no willpower needed)
- 5:50 — Get dressed (same rotation of outfits, don't think about it)
- 6:00 — Coffee + breakfast (prep the night before if possible)
- 6:20 — NOW check phone, respond to messages
- 6:35 — Out the door

The big move is keeping the phone away until you've done the physical stuff. Would something like that work with your setup?

honestly thats close to how I used to operate. I did 4 years in the Marines out of high school and back then there was no laying around. you just get up and do the thing. somewhere along the way I got soft about it

Man, if you've got that Marine discipline baked in, you don't need to learn anything new — you just need to flip that switch back on. The muscle memory is there.

The difference between then and now is probably accountability. In the Corps, someone was making sure you were up and moving. Now it's just you and a warm bed and a phone full of distractions.

Two things that might help you get back to that mode:

1. **Put your phone in another room at night.** Use a $10 alarm clock. If the phone isn't in your hand, you can't scroll.
2. **Give yourself a hard "feet on floor" rule.** Alarm goes off, feet hit the ground, no negotiation. You know how to do this — you did it for 4 years.

The routine I laid out above is basically just boot camp lite. Same principle: set sequence, no thinking, just execute.
the phone in another room thing is smart. thats honestly the whole problem right there. I pick it up and 20 minutes disappears

Exactly. That phone is engineered by billion-dollar companies to keep you staring at it. It's not a willpower problem — it's a design problem. Remove the temptation entirely.

Grab a cheap alarm clock today. Plug the phone in the kitchen or bathroom tonight. Tomorrow morning you'll be amazed how much faster you move when there's nothing to look at.

The first 2-3 days will feel weird. After that it's just how you wake up. You'll probably be out the door by 6:30 without even trying.
what about the messages though. I got 3 guys that work for me and sometimes they text early about being late or whatever. I need to see that stuff

Fair point — you can't go dark when you've got employees. Here's the fix:

Phone still goes in another room, but you check it at one specific time — right after you shower and get dressed. So around 6:00. That's still well before any of your guys would need an answer, and you've already knocked out the important stuff.

The key is: walk to the phone, check messages, respond to anything urgent, then PUT IT DOWN. Don't open anything else. Treat it like a tool, not entertainment.

If someone has a real emergency at 5:30 AM, they'll call, not text. And your phone will ring loud enough from the other room. You're not missing anything critical in that 30-minute window.
yeah thats true. nobody's texting me anything at 530 that cant wait til 6. alright Im doing this tonight

Good. Don't overthink it — just do it. Buy the alarm clock on the way home, plug the phone in the kitchen, and see what tomorrow morning feels like.

If you want to go all-in, lay out your clothes tonight and have breakfast figured out too. Eliminate every decision you can so the morning just runs on autopilot.

You already know how to do this. Just need to get back to it.
appreciate it brother. simple stuff but sometimes you need somebody to spell it out

That's all it is — simple stuff done consistently. With your background, I'd bet money you're out the door by 6:30 within a week.

Let me know how it goes. Good luck tomorrow morning.
